intro:
Atonement (English "at-one-ment") is the reconciliation of sinful humanity to God through the removal of guilt and the restoration of fellowship. The Hebrew kipper ("to cover, ransom, make atonement") expresses how the holy God forgives sin justly — not by overlooking it, but by dealing with it through a substitute.

The Old Testament foreshadows it in the sacrificial system: atonement is made by blood, "for the life of the flesh is in the blood… it is the blood that makes atonement by the life" (Leviticus 17:11). On the Day of Atonement two goats — one slain, one bearing the people’s sins away into the wilderness — portrayed both payment and removal (Leviticus 16:21–22); the Passover lamb’s blood shielded from judgment (Exodus 12:13); and the Servant was "pierced for our transgressions," the LORD laying on him "the iniquity of us all" (Isaiah 53:5–6).

Christ’s death is the true and final atonement, which Scripture describes from several angles: propitiation, turning away God’s wrath (Romans 3:25; 1 John 4:10); substitution, the Lamb of God who "bore our sins in his body" (John 1:29; 1 Peter 2:24); ransom/redemption, his life given "as a ransom for many" (Mark 10:45); reconciliation, God "reconciling the world to himself" (2 Corinthians 5:18–21); and victory over the powers (Colossians 2:15).

Hebrews unites these in high-priestly terms: Christ is both priest and sacrifice, who "entered once for all into the holy places… by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption" (Hebrews 9:11–14) and "by a single offering… has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified" (Hebrews 10:12–14). At the cross he declared "It is finished" (John 19:30) — the complete and sufficient ground of forgiveness, peace with God, and access to him.
